Question: State whether the given sums are equal or unequal. $$\sum_{i = 1}^{10} i$$ and $$\sum_{k = 1}^{10} k$$ $$\sum_{i = 1}^{10} i$$ and $$\sum_{i = 6}^{15} ( i - 5 )$$ $$\sum_{i = 1}^{10} i ( i - 1 )$$ and $$\sum_{j = 0}^{9} ( j + 1 ) j$$ $$\sum_{i = 1}^{10} i ( i - 1 )$$ and $$\sum_{k = 1}^{10} ( k^{2} - k )$$
Answer: a. They are equal; both represent the sum of the first 10 whole numbers. b. They are equal; both represent the sum of the first 10 whole numbers. c. They are equal by substituting $$j = i - 1 .$$ d. They are equal; the first sum factors the terms of the second.

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, graph the function then use a calculator or a computer program to evaluate the following left and right endpoint sums. If the two agree, say "neither."
Question: [T] L 100 and R 100 for $$y = x^{2}$$ on the interval $$[ 0 , 1 ]$$
Answer: $$R_{100} = 0.33835 , L_{100} = 0.32835 .$$ The plot shows that the left Riemann sum is an underestimate because the function is increasing. Similarly, the right Riemann sum is an overestimate. The area lies between the left and right Riemann sums. Ten rectangles are shown for visual clarity. This behavior persists for more rectangles.

\centering
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|}
\hline
Decade & Ppm/y \\ \hline
1964-1973 & 1.07 \\ \hline
1974-1983 & 1.34 \\ \hline
1984-1993 & 1.40 \\ \hline
1994-2003 & 1.87 \\ \hline
2004-2013 & 2.07 \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table} Table 1.2 Average Annual Atmospheric CO 2 Increase, 1964-2013 Source : http://www.esrl.noaa.gov/gmd/ccgg/trends/.

Question:
The following table gives the approximate increase in sea level in inches over 20 years starting in the given year. Estimate the net change in mean sea level from 1870 to 2010. \begin{table}[h]
\centering
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|}
\hline
Starting Year & 20-Year Change \\ \hline
1870 & 0.3 \\ \hline
1890 & 1.5 \\ \hline
1910 & 0.2 \\ \hline
1930 & 2.8 \\ \hline
1950 & 0.7 \\ \hline
1970 & 1.1 \\ \hline
1990 & 1.5 \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table} Table 1.3 Approximate 20-Year Sea Level Increases, 1870-1990 Source : http://link.springer.com/article/10.1007%2Fs10712-011-9119-1
Answer: Add the numbers to get 8.1-in. net increase.

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use a calculator or a computer program to evaluate the endpoint sums R N and L N for $$N = 1,10,100 .$$ How do these estimates compare with the exact answers, which you can find via geometry?

Answer: $$R_{1} = -1 , L_{1} = 1 , R_{10} = -0.1 , L_{10} = 0.1 , L_{100} = 0.01 ,$$ and $$R_{100} = -0.1 .$$ By symmetry of the graph, the exact area is zero.

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use a calculator or a computer program to evaluate the endpoint sums R N and L N for $$N = 1,10,100 .$$
Question: Explain why, if $$f ( a ) \geq 0$$ and f is increasing on $$[ a , b ] ,$$ that the left endpoint estimate is a lower bound for the area below the graph of f on $$[ a , b ] .$$
Answer: If $$[ c , d ]$$ is a subinterval of $$[ a , b ]$$ under one of the left-endpoint sum rectangles, then the area of the rectangle contributing to the left-endpoint estimate is $$f ( c ) ( d - c ) .$$ But, $$f ( c ) \le f ( x )$$ for $$c \le x \le d ,$$ so the area under the graph of f between c and d is $$f ( c ) ( d - c )$$ plus the area below the graph of f but above the horizontal line segment at height $$f ( c ) ,$$ which is positive. As this is true for each left-endpoint sum interval, it follows that the left Riemann sum is less than or equal to the area below the graph of f on $$[ a , b ] .$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use a calculator or a computer program to evaluate the endpoint sums R N and L N for $$N = 1,10,100 .$$
Question: Explain why, if $$f ( b ) \geq 0$$ and f is decreasing on $$[ a , b ] ,$$ that the left endpoint estimate is an upper bound for the area below the graph of f on $$[ a , b ] .$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use a calculator or a computer program to evaluate the endpoint sums R N and L N for $$N = 1,10,100 .$$
Question: Show that, in general, $$R_{N} - L_{N} = ( b - a ) \times \frac{f ( b ) - f ( a )}{N} .$$
Answer: $$L_{N} = \frac{b - a}{N} \sum_{i = 1}^{N} f ( a + ( b - a ) \frac{i - 1}{N} ) = \frac{b - a}{N} \sum_{i = 0}^{N - 1} f ( a + ( b - a ) \frac{i}{N} )$$ and $$R_{N} = \frac{b - a}{N} \sum_{i = 1}^{N} f ( a + ( b - a ) \frac{i}{N} ) .$$ The left sum has a term corresponding to $$i = 0$$ and the right sum has a term corresponding to $$i = N .$$ In $$R_{N} - L_{N} ,$$ any term corresponding to $$i = 1 , 2 \text{,unicode[:ddot.],} N - 1$$ occurs once with a plus sign and once with a minus sign, so each such term cancels and one is left with $$R_{N} - L_{N} = \frac{b - a}{N} ( f ( a + ( b - a ) ) \frac{N}{N} ) - ( f ( a ) + ( b - a ) \frac{0}{N} ) = \frac{b - a}{N} ( f ( b ) - f ( a ) ) .$$

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use a calculator or a computer program to evaluate the endpoint sums R N and L N for $$N = 1,10,100 .$$
Question: Explain why, if f is increasing on $$[ a , b ] ,$$ the error between either L N or R N and the area A below the graph of f is at most $$( b - a ) \frac{f ( b ) - f ( a )}{N} .$$

Obtain an upper bound $$U ( A )$$ for the area by adding to $$L ( A )$$ the areas $$B ( A )$$ of the squares enclosed partially by the curve.

Problem Statement: In the following exercises, use a calculator or a computer program to evaluate the endpoint sums R N and L N for $$N = 1,10,100 .$$
Question: A unit circle is made up of n wedges equivalent to the inner wedge in the figure. The base of the inner triangle is 1 unit and its height is $$\text{sin} ( \frac{2 \pi}{n} ) .$$ The base of the outer triangle is $$B = \text{cos} ( \frac{\pi}{n} ) + \text{sin} ( \frac{\pi}{n} ) \text{tan} ( \frac{\pi}{n} )$$ and the height is $$H = B \text{sin} ( \frac{2 \pi}{n} ) .$$ Use this information to argue that the area of a unit circle is equal to π .
Answer: Let A be the area of the unit circle. The circle encloses n congruent triangles each of area $$\frac{\text{sin} ( \frac{2 \pi}{n} )}{2} ,$$ so $$\frac{n}{2} \text{sin} ( \frac{2 \pi}{n} ) \le A .$$ Similarly, the circle is contained inside n congruent triangles each of area $$\frac{B H}{2} = \frac{1}{2} ( \cos{( \frac{\mathrm{\pi}}{n} )} + \sin{} ( \frac{\mathrm{\pi}}{n} ) \tan{} ( \frac{\mathrm{\pi}}{n} ) )^{2} \sin{} ( \frac{2 \mathrm{\pi}}{n} ) ,$$ so $$A \le \frac{n}{2} \text{sin} ( \frac{2 \pi}{n} ) ( \text{cos} ( \frac{\pi}{n} ) + \text{sin} ( \frac{\pi}{n} ) \text{tan} ( \frac{\pi}{n} ) )^{2} .$$ As $$n \vec{} \infty , \frac{n}{2} \text{sin} ( \frac{2 \pi}{n} ) = \frac{\text{sin} ( \frac{2 \pi}{n} )}{( \frac{2}{n} )} \upAlpha \vec{} \pi ,$$ so we conclude $$\pi \le A .$$ Also, as $$n \vec{} \infty , \text{cos} ( \frac{\pi}{n} ) + \text{sin} ( \frac{\pi}{n} ) \text{tan} ( \frac{\pi}{n} ) \vec{} 1 ,$$ so we also have $$A \le \pi .$$ By the squeeze theorem for limits, we conclude that $$A = \pi .$$
